stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] The nurse is caring for a 72-year-old client with pneumonia whose condition is declining despite initial interventions. Based on the clinical data, complete the bowtie to identify the client's priority condition, the two most important actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

options.hoverRationale: Norepinephrine, a potent alpha-1 agonist, causes peripheral vasoconstriction. This counteracts the systemic vasodilation of septic shock, increasing systemic vascular resistance and raising mean arterial pressure to improve organ perfusion.
This client is in distributive shock with relative hypovolemia. Administering a diuretic would cause further fluid loss, worsening hypotension and exacerbating tissue hypoperfusion.

CVP measurement reflects right atrial pressure, providing an estimate of preload and fluid volume status. This data is essential to guide further fluid resuscitation and prevent volume overload while administering vasopressors.

Sepsis-induced vasodilation and capillary leak lead to distributive shock. Persistent hypotension despite adequate fluid resuscitation, coupled with evidence of organ dysfunction (high lactate, creatinine), confirms this diagnosis.
While the client has pneumonia and is at risk for ARDS, the immediate life-threatening condition is circulatory collapse from septic shock. Hemodynamic stabilization is the priority.

This client's warm, flushed skin and hypotension refractory to fluids are classic for distributive (septic) shock. Cardiogenic shock typically presents with signs of pump failure, such as cool, clammy skin.

MAP represents the average pressure in the arteries and is the most critical indicator of organ perfusion pressure. Titrating vasopressors to a target MAP (≥65 mmHg) is a primary goal in shock management.

Renal perfusion is highly sensitive to changes in systemic blood pressure. Hourly urine output is a direct and immediate indicator of kidney function and overall end-organ perfusion, reflecting resuscitation adequacy.
While important for guiding long-term antibiotic therapy, monitoring sputum characteristics is not a priority for assessing immediate hemodynamic stability or the response to vasopressor therapy.
Fever is a sign of the underlying infection, but temperature is not a direct measure of perfusion or hemodynamic stability. Correcting the circulatory collapse is the immediate life-saving priority.

clinicalPearls: Septic shock is defined by persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain a MAP ≥ 65 mmHg and a serum lactate level > 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation.
Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock due to its potent alpha-adrenergic effects, which increase systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure.
Hourly urine output is a critical, real-time indicator of end-organ perfusion, particularly renal perfusion, during shock resuscitation. A goal is typically > 0.5 mL/kg/hr.
Central venous pressure (CVP) helps guide fluid therapy by estimating preload, but it should be interpreted in the context of the entire clinical picture, not as an isolated value.

questionTrap.trapDescription: The trap is focusing on the client's respiratory symptoms from pneumonia and selecting ARDS as the primary condition. While related, the immediate life-threatening problem is circulatory collapse from septic shock.
questionTrap.strategy: Prioritize the ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation). The client's profound hypotension and signs of hypoperfusion (high lactate, low MAP) indicate a critical circulation problem that must be addressed immediately to prevent multi-organ failure.

mnemonic.backsideText: L-actate measurement; O-btain blood cultures; A-dminister broad-spectrum antibiotics; F-luid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid); V-asopressors if hypotensive during/after fluids.

content.text:
S: This is the ICU nurse for the 72-year-old male in Bed 4 with pneumonia and septic shock.
B: He has a history of COPD and HTN with a penicillin allergy. He was admitted at 04:00 for sepsis.
A: At 05:00, despite a 1.5L 0.9% sodium chloride bolus, his BP remains 80/50 mmHg (MAP 60). He is lethargic, tachycardic at 115, with a lactate of 4.2 mmol/L and creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL.
R: Recommend immediate initiation of the norepinephrine infusion to titrate MAP to ≥ 65 mmHg and continue hourly CVP and urine output monitoring.

tabs.title: Vitals & Telemetry
tabs.content:
TimeTempHRRRBP (MAP)SpO₂
04:00103.1°F (39.5°C)1202880/50 (60) mmHg90% on 2L NC
04:30102.9°F (39.4°C)1182685/55 (65) mmHg92% on 2L NC
05:00102.7°F (39.3°C)1152480/50 (60) mmHg93% on 2L NC

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Lethargic, appears acutely ill. Arousable to loud verbal stimuli. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic with S1/S2 regular. Pulses +1 peripherally. Capillary refill >3 seconds. No murmurs or gallops auscultated. Respiratory: Labored breathing. Coarse crackles auscultated in bilateral lower lobes. Productive cough with purulent green sputum. Increased work of breathing noted with accessory muscle use. Neurological: GCS 13 (E4, V4, M5). Oriented to person only. Intermittently confused. Pupils equal and reactive to light. Skin: Warm, flushed, dry mucous membranes. No edema noted.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Type: Chest X-ray (Portable AP) Date: Day 1, 04:45 Findings: Lungs show diffuse, bilateral opacities, more pronounced in the lower lobes. No evidence of pneumothorax or significant pleural effusion. Cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits. Impression: Findings are consistent with multifocal pneumonia and possible early ARDS. Recommend further evaluation for ARDS progression.

tabs.title: MAR Console
tabs.content:
MedicationDoseRouteFrequencyLast Administered
Norepinephrine2 mcg/min (titrate to MAP > 65 mmHg)IV InfusionContinuousN/A (New Order)
Vancomycin1.5 gIVq12hDay 1, 04:15
Cefepime2 gIVq8hDay 1, 04:15
0.9% Sodium Chloride1000 mL bolusIVSTATDay 1, 04:10
0.9% Sodium Chloride500 mL bolusIVSTATDay 1, 04:40
Pantoprazole40 mgIVq24hDay 1, 05:10

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Priority Nursing Diagnoses:
  • Decreased Cardiac Output related to vasodilation and increased capillary permeability as evidenced by hypotension (MAP < 65 mmHg) and tachycardia.
  • Impaired Gas Exchange related to pneumonia as evidenced by coarse crackles, labored breathing, and SpO2 < 93% on 2L NC.
  • Risk for Infection Spread related to infectious process as evidenced by elevated WBC and purulent sputum.
Nursing Interventions:
  • Administer norepinephrine infusion and titrate to maintain MAP ≥ 65 mmHg. Monitor BP and HR every 5-15 minutes during titration.
  • Administer prescribed antibiotics (Vancomycin and Cefepime) as ordered. Monitor for adverse reactions.
  • Monitor hourly urine output and CVP to assess fluid status and guide fluid resuscitation.
  • Maintain strict aseptic technique during all procedures to prevent further infection.
  • Elevate head of bed to 30-45 degrees to promote lung expansion. Encourage coughing and deep breathing exercises.
